coredata.dc:title: The reconstruction of char surface by oxidized quantum-size carbon dots under the ultrasonic energy to prepare modified activated carbon materials as electrodes for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Leng, Changyu
Sun, Kang
Li, Jihui
Jiang, Jianchun
coredata.dc:description: The traditional oxidation of activated carbons (ACs) by chemical oxidants has many defects, such as the destruction of pore nanostructures and the specific surface area, the formation of unstable oxygen functional groups, which can cause negative effects, especially as electrodes for the aqueous supercapacitors, they can also enhance the capacitance fading, and the pseudo-capacitance is not stable. A new strategy was devised to prepare modified AC with the stable surface properties and developed porosity that can solve these problems. Hydrogen peroxide, as a cheap and clean oxidant, was used to modify the biomass-based chars under the ultrasonic energy, so as to improve the chemical and physical properties of ACs. The whole process can be considered as a reconstruction of the carbon surface, the quantum-size carbon dots (CDs) can be released under the ultrasonic energy from the char surface and combined with oxygen free radical species in the liquid phase and eventually return onto the surface. The recombination of free radical species and CDs can not only form stable oxygen groups, but also create more active sites of chars to form the channels, which can generate surface pores of chars before the further formation of pores by activation. The modified ACs have stable oxygen groups, great surface wettability, electrical conductivity, and excellent electrochemical performance. It can be understood that the physical&chemical properties and the electrochemical performance of modified ACs can be significantly improved. The above results can promote the development of modified activated carbon materials and the practical application of aqueous supercapacitors for electric vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Char surface
Oxidized quantum-size carbon dots
Ultrasonic energy
Modified activated carbon
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) the corresponding pore size distributions of the AC and UAC samples.

The SEM images of the raw char and hydrogen peroxide modified char samples under ultrasonic energy. (a) The harsh and rough surface of coconut shell-based char. (b) The smooth surface UC1%H2O2 with less defects and organic impurities. (c) The surface of the UC5%H2O2. (d) The porous nanostructures of the UC10%H2O2. (e) and (f) The surface of the C10%H2O2 and UC30%H2O2, which was destroyed by the ultrahigh content of the hydrogen peroxide. The TEM images of the UAC10%H2O2 (g) the intensive porous nanostructures of UAC10%H2O2. (h) the graphitic layers of UAC10%H2O2, and the inset picture was the red cycle part of (h). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The FT-IR spectra of AC and UACs in different contents of hydrogen peroxide.

Characterizations of UAC10%H2O2. (a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of UC and UAC samples. (b) High-resolution XPS analysis of the C 1s peaks of UAC10%H2O2. (c) High resolution XPS spectra of the O 1s peaks of UAC10%H2O2.

UV–vis for oxidized carbon QDs in aqueous solution, the optical photographs of the UAC1%H2O2, UAC10%H2O2, UAC30%H2O2 obtained under excitation at 365 nm. (a) UAC1%H2O2, (b) UAC10%H2O2, (c) UAC30%H2O2. (d) and (e) TEM images of CDs. (f) The size distribution plot of the carbon dots.

Electrochemical performance characteristics of ACs measured in a two-electrode system in 1 M H2SO4. (a) CV curves at the fast scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at high current density of 1 A g−1. (c) Rate performance of AC and UAC1%H2O2,UAC5%H2O2, UAC10%H2O2, UAC20%H2O2, UAC30%H2O2. (d) Nyquist plots of AC and modified UAC samples. (e) CV curves of UAC10%H2O2 at different scan rates. (f) Cycling stability of UAC10%H2O2 at 10 A g−1. (g) Ragone plots of AC and UAC10%H2O2 as electrodes for aqueous supercapacitors.

(1)–(2), the quantum-size carbon dots (CDs) were released from the surface of char, which can combine with the hydrogen peroxide to form oxygen free radical species. (3) the oxygen free radical species can incorporate each other to generate water and oxygen gas. (4) the CDs with oxygen free radical species can reconstruct the surface of chars. (5) the overall reaction, which is similar to the free radical species mechanism.

Two routes of H2O2 surface modification of activated carbon with coconut shell as precursor. Route: (a) The char was mixed with H2O2, then activated with KOH. (b) Add the ultrasonic energy during (a) process. And the concentration range of H2O2 is from 1% to 30%. UC was the char, which was modified under the ultrasonic energy. UAC was the activated carbon, which was modified under the ultrasonic energy.

The chemical and physical properties of chars surface before or after oxidation by hydrogen peroxide with the ultrasonic energy. (a) the surface of chars before oxidation. (b) the surface of chars after oxidation by low content range of hydrogen peroxide (1%, 2%, 5%). (c) the surface of chars after oxidation by proper content range of hydrogen peroxide (10%). (d) the surface of char after oxidation by ultrahigh content range of hydrogen peroxide (20%, 30%).
Characteristics of pores in AC and UAC samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Vme: mesopore volume (2–5 nm); Vmi: micropore volume (0–2 nm).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: C 1s and O 1s analysis of UAC1%H2O2, 10%H2O2, 30%H2O2, UC10%H2O2 and AC.
C1s analysis of UC and UACs.

head.ce:title: The reconstruction of char surface by oxidized quantum-size carbon dots under the ultrasonic energy to prepare modified activated carbon materials as electrodes for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, CAF, National Engineering Lab for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Key and Open Lab on Forest Chemical Engineering, SFA, Nanjing 210042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products
National Engineering Lab for Biomass Chemical Utilization
Key and Open Lab on Forest Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The traditional oxidation of activated carbons (ACs) by chemical oxidants has many defects, such as the destruction of pore nanostructures and the specific surface area, the formation of unstable oxygen functional groups, which can cause negative effects, especially as electrodes for the aqueous supercapacitors, they can also enhance the capacitance fading, and the pseudo-capacitance is not stable. A new strategy was devised to prepare modified AC with the stable surface properties and developed porosity that can solve these problems. Hydrogen peroxide, as a cheap and clean oxidant, was used to modify the biomass-based chars under the ultrasonic energy, so as to improve the chemical and physical properties of ACs. The whole process can be considered as a reconstruction of the carbon surface, the quantum-size carbon dots (CDs) can be released under the ultrasonic energy from the char surface and combined with oxygen free radical species in the liquid phase and eventually return onto the surface. The recombination of free radical species and CDs can not only form stable oxygen groups, but also create more active sites of chars to form the channels, which can generate surface pores of chars before the further formation of pores by activation. The modified ACs have stable oxygen groups, great surface wettability, electrical conductivity, and excellent electrochemical performance. It can be understood that the physical&chemical properties and the electrochemical performance of modified ACs can be significantly improved. The above results can promote the development of modified activated carbon materials and the practical application of aqueous supercapacitors for electric vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demands and challenges of energy storage, there is an urgent need for developing high-performance energy storage devices and advanced functional materials [1–5]. Activated carbon materials (ACs) as electrodes for supercapacitors have attracted lots of attention because of low cost, high chemical and thermal stability, high specific surface area, excellent electrical conductivity and controlled porosity and abundant pores whose size can be adapted to electrolyte ions [6]. Recently, biomass materials have been widely used as precursors to prepare activated carbon materials, which are very renewable, easily available and low cost [7–9]. However, biomass are mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin, which decompose in the low temperature range of 300–500 °C, obtaining chars with a small content of heteroatoms. ACs are usually prepared under a high temperature, so the surface of ACs have the properties of hydrophobicity and chemical inertness. Hence, the aqueous electrolyte ions cannot wet the surface and enter the pores of ACs adequately. This is one of the reasons why ACs usually have limited specific capacitance (100–200 F g−1) and low energy density (5–10 Wh kg−1) no matter how large their specific surface area and total pore volume are as electrodes for aqueous supercapacitors (SCs) [10].

Therefore, it is necessary to modify the chemical and physical properties of the surface of ACs to improve its electrochemical performance. The metal oxides doped ACs attracted widespread interests recently, but they still have some drawbacks (e.g. poor rate capability and cycling stability), which can limit their practical application [11]. Various heteroatoms doping and surface functional groups can also contribute to the high specific capacitance and high energy density [12–15]. Among these surface groups, oxygen groups are the most important because they are abundant and modify the behavior of activated carbons, such as surface wettability, chemical reactivity and electrical conductivity. The chemical oxidation treatments were usually used to add oxygen functional groups onto the surface of activated carbons [16]. According to the previous reports, hydrogen peroxide, as a cheap and clean oxidant, is used to treat activated carbons directly [17]. It can turn into water and oxygen gas after the oxidation process. However, lower H2O2 concentration can enhance oxygen groups of activated carbons, which are unstable and worthless. Higher H2O2 concentration can destroy the pore structure and reduce the specific surface area of activated carbons inevitably, and in all cases the pseudocapacitance properties are not stable and cause fast capacitance fading. Recently, oxygen-rich precursors have been used to prepare ACs with high amounts of oxygen by one-step carbonization process [23]. However, they have the small specific surface area and poorly developed porosity, which resulted in a high leak current. Therefore, a method that can be used to prepare ACs with both optimal nanostructures and stable oxygen groups incorporated in the carbon framework should be provided for aqueous supercapacitors.

In this paper, a new idea and strategy is provided to modify the biomass-based chars, and therefore to achieve the optimal modification of ACs. The hydrogen peroxide was used to treat the char material with the ultrasonic energy, which resulted in an entirely different phenomenon than the traditional oxidation of ACs treated with hydrogen peroxide directly. The ultrasonic energy in the oxidation of chars has three main effects: First, it cleans the organic impurities and defects in the pores and channels of the chars because it can reduce the Van der Waals' force between particles. Second, it can promote the reconstruction of the chars surface and formation of stable oxygen groups, which can provide a pseudocapacitance in aqueous electrolyte stably. Finally, it can promote the release of quantum-size carbon dots from the char surface and the controllable return during the oxidation reactions, which can then form stable oxygen doping and pores in carbon frameworks. The released carbon dots under ultrasonic energy can combine with oxygen free radical species to form oxidized carbon dots, which can be prove by the following proofs: (1) the reaction mechanism, (2) analysis of test results, (3) the intrinsic properties of carbon dots. So the as-obtained ACs had the high specific surface area, large total pore volume, stable and high content of oxygen groups, great electrical conductivity. The disadvantages of hydrogen peroxide during the traditional oxidation were transformed into optimal conditions to modify chars. Furthermore, the oxygen groups are not only attached to the surface of chars, they are combined with quantum-size carbon dots as oxygen free radical species to reconstruct the surface during the modification. The oxygen atoms can be incorporated into the carbon framework stably and uniformly. Compared with the traditional chemical method, the presence of oxygen can significantly improve the chemical and thermal stability. So the oxygen groups of chars can be kept after high temperature activation.

The oxidation reaction takes place through an electron-transfer reaction similar to the free radical mechanism as shown in Scheme 1
[18]. The reactions can be considered as a reconstruction of the carbon surface. The quantum-size carbon dots (CDs) can be released under the ultrasonic energy from the surface to combine with oxygen free radical species, which are provided by hydrogen peroxide, in the liquid phase or onto the surface of chars. The CDs can be facilely fabricated by chemical oxidation or cutting strategies, so the carbocyclic rings are easily opened to combine with oxygen doping. [19–21] The oxidized CDs also have rich oxygen functional groups at the edge, which provide great aqueous dispersity and subsequent surface functionality. [22] So the chars have rich oxygen surface functional groups and stable chemical bonds with oxygen incorporated in carbon frameworks after oxidation. The recombination of free radical species and carbon dots also create more active sites and pores of chars to form the channels, which can generate surface pores of chars before the further formation of pores by activation. So the oxidation can not only improve the surface properties of ACs, but also benefit the developed porosity. In this paper, a novel strategy is presented to modify biomass-based chars using affordable hydrogen peroxide to clean the surface and release carbon dots under ultrasonic energy in order to reconstruct the char surface and offer stable oxygen species. The UAC samples exhibit promising electrochemical performances as electrodes for aqueous supercapacitors.

There were two methods to modify chars to prepare activated carbons. coconut shell was used as raw materials to prepare char in the temperature of 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1. Hydrogen peroxide was used as the oxidation reagent to treat the 5 g chars for oxidation with 100 ml 0%, 1%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 30% hydrogen peroxide under the ultrasonic energy for 1 h when the ultrasonic power was 100 W, respectively. The 100 ml 10% hydrogen peroxide and 5 g char were particularly matching 1:1 M ratio. They were washed and dried at 120 °C for 12 h. The chars were mixed with potassium hydroxide for activation at 1:4 wt in a covered stainless steel vessel and emptied into a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the temperature of reactor was raised up to 800 °C for 1 h to prepare AC. The char was also modified by hydrogen peroxide without the ultrasonic energy for comparation. The as-obtained samples were prepared and denoted as shown in Scheme 2 .

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to observe the variation tendency of surface morphology and porous nanostructures. The porous texture of ACs was also analyzed by N2 adsorption (77 K) and the samples were degassed at 300 °C in a vacuum for 15 h before all the gas adsorption measurements. The specific surface area (SSA) was calculated from the N2 isotherm with p/p0 in the range 0.1–0.25 by applying BET method. The SSA and pore size distribution (PSD) were also calculated by the DFT equation using N2 adsorption data. The total pore volume was confirmed by the amount of nitrogen adsorbed (p/p0 = 0.9). The average pore sizes of micropores and mesopores were estimated by the DFT method with the p/p0 values between 10−7-0.1 and 0.1–0.99, respectively. The oxygen groups on the surface of chars and ACs were verified by FTIR technique, which was conducted on an AVATER-306B FTIR spectrometer in the range from 400 to 4000. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were also carried out to analyze the surface atomic content. The conductivity of ACs were measured by the standard four-probe method with a thickness of 1 mm and a diameter of 10 mm prepared under a pressure of 7.1 M Pa.

The electrodes were prepared by mixing 5 wt.% polytetrafluoroethylene, 10 wt.% acetylene black and 85 wt.% ACs and grounding in a mortar. The slurry was then pressed on a stainless steel to form the working electrode in which 6 mg of active material is contained. The thickness of electrode material was 100 μm, the electrode area was 1 cm2. All the electrochemical tests were carried out with coin-type cells (CR2032). Full cells were assembled in the coin-type cells with the same carbon as the negative electrode, glass fiber as separator, and 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. All the electrochemical performances were tested by a VMP3 instrument (Bio-Logic, France). The CV scan rate was in a range of 5–100 mV s−1 and the voltage window for ionic liquid and aqueous electrolyte was set as 0–2.5 and 0–1 V, respectively. The GC curves were measured under the current density ranging from 0.1 to 20 A g−1 and EIS was performed at frequencies from 100 kHz to 10 MHz. The energy and power densities (Ragone plots) were evaluated from the galvanostatic discharge curves by taking account the total mass of both electrode active materials. The gravimetric capacitance of samples was calculated from CV curves by two-electrode system, according to the equation,
C =
I Δ t
m Δ V

The gravimetric capacitance of samples was also calculated from GCD curves by three-electrode system, according to the formula,
C =
2 I Δ t
m Δ V

For CV curves, the ΔV/Δt means the scan rate, I refers the response current. For GCD curves, Δt is the discharging time, ΔV is the voltage and I denotes the current. In both case, m is the mass of the active material on single electrode. The energy density was calculated according to the equation:
E =
C V
8
2

C denotes the electrode specific capacitance and V is the cell voltage after at the beginning of the discharge. The power density was estimated using the equation:
P =
E
t d
where td is the discharging time.

The porous texture of ACs were shown in Fig. 1 , according to IUPAC classification, the isotherms of all AC samples were type I that there were a large amount of micropores of it. It indicated that the modified UACs had abundant small mesopores in Fig. 1b and Table 1 , which can provide the short ions diffusion pathway for fast ion transfer. The UACs had more mesopores than AC because the hydrogen peroxide can improve the surface wettability and the chemical reactivity of char surface, which can benefit the further activation. The UAC10%H2O2 showed an obviously higher isotherm than AC, it proved that the ultrasonic energy also had some positive effects during the oxidation, such as that the ultrasonic energy can deeply clean the organic impurities blocked in the pores and channels by reducing the intermolecular forces between particles. What's more, the UAC10%H2O2 exhibited the highest isotherm than other samples because its char had some pores on the surface that K+ can enter the internal channels adequately during the activation. The UAC30%H2O2 showed lower isotherms than the UAC10%H2O2 and its N2 adsorption volumes showed a decrease at the relative pressure of 0.4. It indicated that the content of hydrogen peroxide also had an appropriate range, instead, the ultrahigh content of hydrogen peroxide can destroy the pores and the specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 1b, the UAC samples had an intensive pore size distribution range from 2 nm to 5 nm and they also had abundant micropores for energy storage. From Table 1, it can be seen that the pore size distribution of UAC10%H2O2 was around 3 nm and the ratio of mesopores is 57%. The mesopore and micropore volumes of UAC10%H2O2 are 0.91 and 0.69 cm3 g−1.

It can be seen in Table 1 that the specific surface areas were 2198, 2522, 3227 and 2700 m2 g−1 with the total pore volumes of 1.11, 1.16, 1.60 and 1.31 cm3 g−1 for AC, UAC1%H2O2, UAC10%H2O2 and UAC30%H2O2, respectively. What's more, the hydrogen peroxide can improve the surface wettability of the char, so the chemical agent can enter the internal channels of the carbon framework well and create more pores and specific surface area. It was the reason that the physical properties increased with the rising content of hydrogen peroxide from 1% to 10%. The excessive content of 30% can destroy the structure of the chars, so the obtained UAC30%H2O2 had a poorer nanostructure than UAC10%H2O2. The average pore size and total volume of UACs was larger than AC because the surface of modified chars was reconstructed by the combination of quantum-size carbon dots and oxygen free radical species under the ultrasonic energy. The modified chars had the surface with great surface wettability, chemical reactivity and pores before the further introduction of pores by activation. The modification of hydrogen peroxide with the ultrasonic energy to treat char materials overcame the defects of the traditional oxidation for activated carbon materials and turned them into advantages.

The surface morphology and pore structure of chars and ACs were analyzed in Fig. 2 by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM), respectively. It can be seen in Fig. 2a that the coconut shell-based char had the rough and harsh surface, which was covered by impurities and non-carbonated particles. The modified char with 1% hydrogen peroxide under the ultrasonic energy (UC1%H2O2) was shown in Fig. 2b and its surface was smooth and most of the impurities had been cleaned. The UC5%H2O2 surface was cleaner and more smooth, which had some slightly hollowed areas as shown in Fig. 2c. The UC10%H2O2 surface had obvious pores as shown in Fig. 2d and the chemical and physical properties of surface were changed dramatically compared with the char. However, as shown in Fig. 2e, the surface of C10%H2O2, which was modified by hydrogen peroxide without the ultrasonic energy, had the entirely different morphology compared with the surface of UC10%H2O2. The char surface can release quantum-size carbon dots under the ultrasonic energy, which was relatively ordered and can be controlled by the content of the hydrogen peroxide. The raised parts on the surface first entered into the liquid phase because of the tip effect and the different contents of hydrogen peroxide can result in different surface morphologies in Scheme 3 . It can be seen in Fig. 2f that the surface of UC30%H2O2 had the destruction with an excessive concentration of hydrogen peroxide. The ultrasonic energy played an important role during the oxidation process, it made the carbon particles dispersed homogeneously in the liquid phase and removed the impurities effectively to obtain the char with pure carbon framework. The TEM images of UAC10%H2O2 were shown in Fig. 2g–h that the porous nanostructures and graphitic layers can be found.

The FT-IR spectra of modified chars and activated carbons with different contents of the hydrogen peroxide was shown in Fig. 3 . The characteristic peak at the range from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 was discovered, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO(H) group. The oxidation can be certified by the observation of characteristic peak about 1750 and 1600 cm−1, which was from the stretching vibration of CO from lactone, carboxyl acid and carbonyl groups. The stretching vibration of CO of ether, phenol and carboxylic anhydride groups at the range from 1250 cm−1 was from the chemical bonds with oxygen doping incorporated in the carbon framework. The stretching vibration of OH, CO and CO became stronger with the increase of the content of hydrogen peroxide from 1% to 10%. The related characteristic peaks were weaker after 10%, which indicated that the excessive content of hydrogen peroxide can generate abundant quantum-size carbon dots, so the carbon framework of the char was destroyed irreversibly and cannot be reconstructed back to the char surface.

The elemental analysis exhibits the content of C and O in chars and activated carbon samples by XPS measurements in Fig. 4 . For the UC10%H2O2, UAC10%H2O2, UAC1%H2O2, UAC30%H2O2 and AC, the content of oxygen groups was 16.09, 14.36, 11.05, 10.56, 8.91 at.% in Table 2 , respectively. It can prove that the released carbon dots under ultrasonic energy could combine with oxygen free radical species to form oxidized carbon dots. The UC10%H2O2 is the char before KOH activation, so it has a high oxygen group content. It can be confirmed that the content of oxygen groups increased with the content of hydrogen peroxide from 1% to 10%. The oxygen groups of UAC30%H2O2 had the dramatical decline, because excessive concentration of hydrogen peroxide can generate more oxidized quantum-size carbon dots in the aqueous solution. The surface of char was destroyed and cannot be reconstructed to form carbon framework, which has been also showed in the FT-IR spectra. The formation of oxidized QCDs can be also proved by the optical photographs of UV–vis under 365 nm. So the UAC30%H2O2 had the lowest content of oxygen groups in the modified UACs.

The variation trends of different oxygen groups have been showed in Table 3 . The content of COC increased with the content of hydrogen peroxide from 1% to 10%. It indicated that the existence form of oxygen in carbon framework was CO chemical bonds and oxygen-doping, which was very stable and abundant. Compared with the UC10%H2O2, after the activation, the oxygen groups of the UAC10%H2O2 were still remained 90 percent under a high temperature. Compared with the AC, the content of oxygen groups of the UAC10%H2O2 rose by 62 percent. The electrical conductivity of UAC10%H2O2 was 18.5 S cm−1, which was much higher than 10 S cm−1 of AC. It was indicated that the oxygen groups of the char, which were formed by oxidized QCDs during the reconstruction of the carbon framework under the ultrasonic energy, were stable and can be retained to prepare oxidized ACs.

The ultrasonic energy can generate more oxidized carbon QDs, which can also be proved by Fig. 5 . And it can be clearly seen that the content of oxidized quantum-size carbon dots increased with the content of the hydrogen peroxide (30% > 10%>1%). It can be seen in Fig. 5d and e that the size of carbon dots is mainly 3–4 and 5–6 nm. The size distribution of carbon dots was exhibited in Fig. 5f [24], and the average pore size of carbon dots is 4.31 nm. The surface of chars can be reconstructed orderly and modified by chemical oxidation or cutting strategies easily. The CDs can combine with oxygen free radical species to form oxidized carbon dots and incorporate onto the surface and in the carbon framework. CDs also had rich oxygen functional groups at the edge, which provided great aqueous dispersity and subsequent surface functionalization. The nanostructures and oxygen functional groups were stable and can retain after the high temperature activation to obtain modified ACs according to the above XPS results.

The electrochemical performance of ACs as electrodes for aqueous supercapacitors was characterized by cyclic voltammetry measurements in Fig. 6 a. The CV curves of UACs all showed almost symmetrical rectangular shapes even at 50 mV s−1, indicating an excellent electrochemical behavior. And it can be seen that UAC10%H2O2 had peaks on the curves around 0.4 V in Fig. 6e, because there were some oxygen groups on the surface and oxygen chemical bonds incorporated in the carbon framework, which generated the stable pseudocapacitance during the charge-discharge process. In addition, the UAC1%H2O2 and UAC5%H2O2 both had more smooth CV curves than the UAC20%H2O2 and UAC30%H2O2, because the lower content of hydrogen peroxide mainly benefited the physical properties of the chars and generated few oxygen groups. It can be clearly seen that all curves showed the optimal rectangular shapes even at the very fast scan rate of 50 mV s−1. And all UAC samples had the better curves than the AC.

As shown in Fig. 6b, all the samples can be charged and discharged smoothly with the typical triangle curves at a high current density of 1 A g−1 and the UAC10%H2O2 showed a longer discharge time and smaller IR drop than the other samples. It might be due to the excellent surface wettability and high efficiency surface area, which can provide pores for energy storage and channels for rapid ions transfer. The UAC10%H2O2 had excellent performance with a high specific capacitance 487 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, and the AC only had the specific capacitance of 296 F g−1. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance of UAC1%H2O2, 5%H2O2, 10%H2O2, 20%H2O2 and 30%H2O2 were 326 (179 F cm−3), 332 (183 F cm−3), 341 (188 F cm−3), 325 (179 F cm−3), 308 F g−1 (169 F cm−3), respectively. These values were higher than AC (265 F g−1) or commercial coconut shell-based activated carbon (200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and were equal or better than those advanced carbon materials recently reported. At the high current density of 2 A g−1, the UAC10%H2O2 can also maintain specific capacitance of 310 F g−1 (91% capacitance retention). However, a capacitance of 210 F g−1 (116 F cm−3) was also retained for UAC10%H2O2 even at 20 A g−1, which made it a promising carbon as electrode for supercapacitor. Compared with the modified UACs, the AC only had a specific capacitance of 80 F g−1 (44 F cm−3) at the current of 10 A g−1. It was due to the stable surface oxygen groups that they provided stable pseudocapacitance, which resulted in high capacitance, high energy density and slow capacitance fading.

Fig. 6c was the rate performance of AC and UAC samples as electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. The specific capacitance of the UAC1%H2O2, 5%H2O2, 10%H2O2 showed slower fading rate than UAC20%H2O2, 30%H2O2. It was due to the appropriate content of oxygen groups, which can improve the chemical and physical properties of the surface, and reduce the resistance in pores and channels, slow the fading capacitance at high scanning rates. Compared with the UAC10%H2O2, the UAC20%H2O2 and 30%H2O2 showed the inferior rate performance and rapid capacitance fading under high current density, which was due to the ultrahigh content of unstable surface oxygen groups and few pores. However, all modified UAC samples had the better rate performance than the AC, which indicated that the UACs had better physical and chemical properties, such as stable oxygen groups and better surface wettability properties than AC. So the UACs had the slower fading rate, less internal resistance and leakage current than AC.

The AC only had the specific capacitance of 15 F g−1 at the large current density of 20 A g−1. However, the specific capacitance of UAC10%H2O2 can still be retained 210 F g−1 at the current density of 20 A g−1. And the UAC10%H2O2 had the specific capacitance of 487 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, which was 1.7 times higher than 296 F g−1 of AC as electrodes in 1 M H2SO4. It indicated that the UACs had the stable oxygen groups after the reconstruction of the char surface, which can provide stable pseudocapacitance and excellent rate performance as electrodes in the aqueous electrolyte. The UAC10%H2O2 also exhibited a capacitance retention of 95% at a high current density of 10 A g−1 after 10000 cycles, which can prove that UAC10%H2O2 had a high-rate performance as electrode with suitable content of oxygen groups.

Fig. 6d showed the Nyquist plots for the AC and modified UACs. Compared with the UAC1%H2O2, 5%H2O2, 20%H2O2 and 30%H2O2, the UAC10%H2O2 showed an almost vertical line in the low frequency region range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz, indicating a good capacitive performance as electrodes for aqueous supercapacitors. The plot of UAC10%H2O2 also showed a straighter slope and shorter transition process, indicating a much lower charge transfer resistance. Furthermore, from the high frequency region, the UAC10%H2O2 had shorter Warburg domain in the plot and smaller semicircle, which proved evidently better ion transfer efficiency and smaller contact resistance between electrolyte solution and electrode material than the other three samples. Above all, the UAC10%H2O2 had the lowest equivalent series resistance (ESR), which was due to its excellent surface wettability, electrical conductivity, interconnected pore and channels. It indicated that the UAC10%H2O2 had better intrinsic electronic properties than other samples, lower ions transfer resistance in the materials and lower contact resistance between electrode and current collector.

It can be clearly seen in Fig. 6e that the CV curve of the UAC10%H2O2 still kept symmetrical rectangular shape even at 100 mV s−1. There were peaks on the curves around 0.4 V at different scan rates, which is due to the pseudocapacitance generated by stable surface oxygen groups. The above results indicated that abundant mesopores of UAC10%H2O2 were suitable for fast ion transport. It can be seen in Fig. 6e that the UAC10%H2O2 had the excellent capacitive performance no matter at high current density or rapid voltage scan rate.

And the UAC10%H2O2 can still remain 95% specific capacitance after 10000 cycles at high current density of 10 A g−1, which exhibits a long-term cycling stability as electrode for supercapacitors in Fig. 6f. The ragone plots of UAC10%H2O2 in Fig. 6g showed that its highest energy density was calculated to be 15.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 1440 W kg−1. The energy density of UAC10%H2O2 was higher than the AC as electrode, which was about 10 Wh kg−1. It can be seen that the modification used in this paper is better than traditional methods and overcomes the disadvantages [25–28].

In this paper, a novel strategy is provided to modify biomass-based chars under ultrasonic energy by hydrogen peroxide, and therefore to change the chemical and physical properties of activated carbons. The as-obtained ACs have excellent surface wettability, electrical conductivity, excellent electrochemical performance as electrode for aqueous supercapacitors. The ultrasonic energy plays an important role during the oxidation that it promotes the release of carbon dots from the surface to the liquid phase combined with oxygen free radical species to form oxidized carbon dots (Avg. = 4.31 nm). Meanwhile, the quantum-size carbon dots can be facilely fabricated by chemical oxidation or cutting strategies. They can form stable oxygen groups and chemical bonds incorporated in the carbon framework, which can remain after high temperature activation and kept on the nanostructure of activated carbon. This work provides a new idea and strategy for the modification of ACs by reconstructing the surface and nanostructure of char materials effectively. The results can also promote the development of carbon materials and the application of aqueous supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the projects in forestry public benefit research sector (201404610).
